core_intuitions: An agent's reported score is a SYSTEM property, not a model property: the number you read = f(model + harness + compute budget + luck). Two leaderboard entries can differ 10-20 points from harness choices alone (file localization, iteration budget, tool suite, context policy).

The SWE-bench evaluation loop is binary and mechanical: apply the agent's git diff, run the task's test suite, PASS only if every test passes; a patch fixing 9 of 10 tests still scores 0, and a non-applying patch short-circuits to FAIL.

pass@k is an unbiased estimator of 'at least one of k samples succeeds'; it climbs with k, and the gap between pass@1 (what a user actually experiences) and pass@k (the ceiling you reach with a perfect verifier) measures the payoff of sampling diversity.

Agent scores are statistically noisy: with ~500 tasks at ~30% solve rate the 95% CI spans about +/-4 points, so a 3-point 'improvement' can be pure noise. The right comparison is a PAIRED test (McNemar on the same task set), which looks only at tasks where the two models disagree.

Outcome scoring (binary pass/fail on final state) is objective but low-signal; trajectory scoring reads the intermediate steps and can distinguish two agents that both failed for very different reasons.

existing.name: ageval-swebench-protocol
existing.issue: In dark theme the entire three-line footer caption ('Score = resolve rate...', 'Step 3 accent-shaded...', 'Timeout during test execution = FAIL') renders as near-black text on the dark background and is unreadable; the diamond 'All tests pass?' label and the tiny 'timeout / patch error' annotation are also very low-contrast in dark. The light-theme version is clear and correct.
existing.improvement: Route all text fills (especially the footer caption block, the diamond label, and the small failure-branch annotations) through theme-aware foreground tokens so they stay legible in both light and dark. Bump the 'timeout / patch error' micro-label to a readable size and ensure it sits fully inside the viewBox rather than crowding the FAIL pill. Keep the accent-shade on Step 3 but make it a semantic-safe accent so the 'agent-authored stage' cue survives dark mode. Structure and reading order are good — do not redraw the pipeline, only fix contrast and the footer legibility.

new_figures.name: ageval-harness-swing
new_figures.concept: The same frozen model produces wildly different benchmark scores depending on harness configuration; the score is a system signal, not a model signal.
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's central thesis, stated in three places ('The harness is part of the system', the entire 'Harness Effects on Scores' section, and the closing 'system signal' point). Readers must internalize that comparing two leaderboard entries compares model+harness, not models. No existing figure conveys this; it is the single most load-bearing intuition of the chapter.
new_figures.brief: Goal: show that ONE fixed model lands at very different places on a resolve-rate axis as you turn harness 'dials'. Layout: a single horizontal score axis running left ('lower resolve rate') to right ('higher resolve rate') spanning the top; use tick marks only, NO invented benchmark numbers on the axis itself. On the left, a small boxed icon labeled 'same model (frozen weights)' with an arrow pointing at the axis. Below the axis, stack 4 labeled harness-dial rows, each a short left-to-right track with 3 stops and an arrow showing which direction lifts the score: (1) File localization: 'agent self-explore -> BM25 top-N -> oracle files'; (2) Iteration budget: '1 loop -> 3 loops -> many (diminishing)'; (3) Tool suite: 'read+edit only -> +run bash / run tests'; (4) Context policy: 'hard truncate -> retrieve / summarize'. Each row's rightward end connects up to a marker on the score axis, so worse configs map to left, richer configs map to right. Overlay a translucent horizontal BAND on the score axis spanning the whole swing, labeled 'the SAME model can land anywhere in this band'. Annotate the band width with the chapter's own documented range as a qualitative caption: 'harness choices routinely move SWE-bench 10-20 points' (this figure quotes the chapter, so the range is legitimate; do not invent finer numbers). Bottom banner (theme-safe text): 'Comparing two leaderboard entries = comparing model + harness, not models. Always disclose the scaffold.' Keep all four dials aligned and legible; use a single semantic accent for the 'lifts score' arrows, neutral tokens elsewhere. Must read clearly as a static frame in both themes.

new_figures.name: ageval-pass-at-k
new_figures.concept: pass@k = probability at least one of k drawn samples is correct; it rises with k, and pass@1 (user experience) sits far below the pass@k ceiling reachable only with a verifier.
new_figures.rationale: pass@k is the chapter's core quantitative tool and has a worked example (n=8, c=2 -> pass@1=0.25, pass@2=0.46, pass@4=0.79). The combinatorial formula is opaque as text; a picture of 'c correct in a pool of n, draw k, chance of at least one hit' plus the rising curve makes the estimator and the pass@1-vs-ceiling gap click. Foundational and not currently visualized.
new_figures.brief: Two-panel static figure using the chapter's own worked example (n=8 samples, c=2 correct) so all numbers are legitimate. LEFT panel ('the sample pool'): draw n=8 sample tiles in a 2x4 grid, 2 marked correct (semantic success accent, small check) and 6 marked failed (neutral). Above them show the estimator in words + formula: 'pass@k = 1 - C(n-c, k)/C(n, k) = P(at least one of k drawn is correct)'. Show a small 'draw k' bracket lifting k tiles out of the pool to illustrate sampling without replacement. RIGHT panel ('pass@k climbs with k'): a rising step/line chart, x-axis k = 1,2,4(,8), y-axis probability 0 to 1, plotting the worked points pass@1=0.25, pass@2=0.46, pass@4=0.79 as labeled dots on the curve. Mark pass@1 with a callout 'what a single run / a real user gets' and the high-k end with a callout 'ceiling reachable ONLY with a perfect verifier to pick the winner'. A thin connector or brace between the two callouts labeled 'sampling-diversity payoff'. Footer note (theme-safe): 'Best-of-k needs a verifier to select the winner; without one, pass@1 is the honest number.' Keep axis labels legible, dots clearly on the curve, both panels balanced. Static frame must fully convey it; do not rely on motion.

new_figures.name: ageval-score-noise-band
new_figures.concept: A few-point score gap can be inside the confidence interval (pure noise); the correct comparison is paired (McNemar), which only counts tasks where the two models disagree.
new_figures.rationale: The statistical-honesty thesis is a pillar of the chapter (Reproducibility & Variance, the CI computation, the Interview Corner: 40% vs 37% is not obviously significant). Readers routinely over-read 1-3 point leaderboard gaps. A figure that (a) shows two overlapping CI bars and (b) shows the paired 2x2 concordance where only the off-diagonal matters makes 'don't compare two point estimates' visceral. Distinct from harness-swing (systematic bias vs random noise) and foundational.
new_figures.brief: Two-part static infographic, all numbers taken as illustrative from the chapter (N=500 tasks, solve rates ~0.30, and the interview example 40% vs 37%; SE ~= sqrt(p(1-p)/N) ~= 0.02 so 95% CI ~= +/-4 pts). TOP part ('point estimates lie'): a horizontal probability axis ~0.20 to 0.50. Plot Model A as a dot at 0.40 and Model B at 0.37, EACH with a 95% CI error bar of roughly +/-0.04. Draw the two error bars so they visibly OVERLAP heavily; shade the overlap region and label it 'the gap lives inside the noise'. Small caption: 'N=500, p~0.30 -> SE~0.02 -> 95% CI ~ +/-4 pts (illustrative)'. BOTTOM part ('compare paired, not raw'): a 2x2 concordance table over the SAME task set with rows 'B passes / B fails' and columns 'A passes / A fails': cells = both-pass, A-only, B-only, both-fail. Grey out the two diagonal cells (both-pass, both-fail) with a label 'ignored — no information'; highlight the two off-diagonal cells (A-only, B-only) with a label 'McNemar looks ONLY here: the disagreements'. Arrow/caption tying them: 'significance depends on A-only vs B-only, not on 40 vs 37'. Footer banner (theme-safe): 'Report CIs and run a paired test (McNemar) on identical tasks; never compare two raw percentages.' Use semantic accents sparingly (one for the overlap, one for the highlighted discordant cells); keep table text legible in both themes and fully inside the viewBox.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: one solid protocol diagram covers SWE-bench scoring, leaving the three pillars of the chapter's measurement-science thesis unvisualized. I keep-with-refine the existing protocol figure (dark-theme footer text is unreadable) and add three foundational statics that map one-to-one onto the core intuitions: harness-swing (score is a system property, systematic bias), pass-at-k (the sampling estimator and the pass@1-vs-ceiling gap), and score-noise-band (random uncertainty + paired vs unpaired comparison). Together they turn the chapter's three quantitative tools into intuition. I deliberately omit a trajectory-vs-outcome figure — it is well handled by prose and the compare_trajectories code, and adding it would dilute the disciplined foundational set.
